title: Accelerated Simulation Algorithms for Extreme First-Passage Problems with General Emission Profiles
abstract: Fastest arrival events, where the first among many diffusing particles reaches a target, are central in triggering signal initiation in molecular stochastic systems. Classical approaches to simulate such events rely on full trajectory generation of all particles, leading to prohibitive computational costs in the large particle number regime. In this work, we present a general simulation framework for efficiently generating order statistics of arrival times by exploiting asymptotic first-passage distributions. This framework applies to diffusion processes in bounded domains with localized absorbing targets, for which short-time first-passage asymptotics are available, such as Brownian motion in dimensions one, two, and three. Starting with the case of instantaneous emission, we derive and implement a recursive inverse transform algorithm to simulate the first $k$ arrivals without tracking particle trajectories. We extend this algorithm to time-dependent emission profiles via an iterative approach, enabling the simulation of extreme statistics in systems with temporal injection, ranging from rapid to prolonged emission. Additionally, we provide asymptotic estimates of the mean fastest arrival time. To conclude, the present acceleration algorithm which bypasses Brownian simulations of trajectories can be used for spatial reaction networks, rare event detection, or diffusion-controlled activation.
summary: This paper introduces a highly efficient algorithm for simulating the earliest arrival events in large populations of diffusing particles. By using asymptotic distributions instead of full trajectory tracking, it achieves orders-of-magnitude speedups for complex scenarios like temporal injection and multi-target competition. This is particularly useful for modeling signal initiation in molecular and biological systems.
main_problem: Simulating extreme first-passage events, where the first among a large population of diffusing particles reaches a target, is computationally prohibitive using standard Brownian dynamics.
main_result: The authors developed an accelerated, trajectory-free simulation framework that generates the first k arrival times with a runtime scaling linearly with k and independent of the total particle number N.
method: A recursive inverse transform algorithm that exploits short-time asymptotic expansions of single-particle survival probabilities and utilizes the Lambert W function for explicit inversion.
model_or_system: Brownian motion of N independent particles in 1D, 2D, and 3D bounded domains with localized absorbing targets, including extensions for time-dependent emission profiles and particle killing.
key_observables: First arrival times, order statistics of arrival times, Mean First Arrival Time (MFAT), and splitting probabilities.
important_parameters: Number of particles (N), number of arrivals (k), diffusion coefficient (D), geodesic distance (delta), emission rate (alpha), and killing rate (gamma).
main_assumptions: The framework assumes the existence of known or computable short-time asymptotic approximations for the single-particle survival probability and that targets are small and well-separated.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the core concept of replacing trajectories with sampling; Figure 2 illustrates the recursive sampling procedure; Figure 3 shows validation against Brownian simulations; Figure 5 compares MFAT in slow/fast regimes; Figure 6 shows the impact of particle killing on arrival distributions.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the computational challenge, presents a recursive algorithm for instantaneous emission, extends the framework to time-dependent injection and particle killing, provides analytical scaling for MFAT, and validates the method against traditional Brownian dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1: Substituting Brownian Simulations with Extreme Escape Statistics. This schematic illustrates the core idea of the algorithm: replacing the computationally intensive simulation of Brownian trajectories up to the first escape event with the independent sampling of two random variables — the arrival time τ and the escape target C — from precomputed probability distributions.

Fig. 2: Iterative Sampling of Successive Escape Events. Recursive procedure used to sim- ulate the first k = 3 escape events: given the arrival time τk of the k-th escape, τk+1 is sampled conditionally, reflecting the decreasing number of particles in the system.

Fig. 3: Arrival Times of the k-th Particle : Stochastic Simulation vs Ac- celerated Algorithm realizations. The markers are averages over simulations and the lines are average over algorithm realizations for the mean (A) and the variance (B) in one dimension.

Fig. 4: Arrival Time Sampling under Uniform Killing. Each Brownian particle is assigned an exponential lifetime. Candidate escape times generated by the recursive inverse transform method are rejected if they exceed the corresponding killing time. Surviving events are retained and sorted by arrival.

Fig. 5: Asymptotic behavior of the MFAT in the slow and fast emission regimes vs accelerated simulations. The slow regime is plotted for N = 1000 in A and B in different scales. The asymptotic approximation (eq. 3.2 dotted curve) agrees well with theoretical formula (eq. 2.4) for small α limit. Discrepancies appear as α →0, due to the approximation of short-time asymptotics for the expression of S(t) beyond their validity range. The fast regime is plotted for N = 1000 in C and D where the relative difference between the fast emission regime and the instantaneous one is shown.
